title: Physics-based modeling of cyclic and calendar aging of LIBs with Si-Gr composite anodes
authors: Micha C. J. Philipp, Lukas Köbbing, Alexander Karger, Andreas Jossen, Arnulf Latz, Birger Horstmann
abstract: Higher energy density and longer lifetime are the requirements for next-generation lithium-ion batteries. A promising anode material is silicon, which offers high specific capacity, but its significant volume change during lithiation and delithiation enormously reduces battery lifetime. A physical understanding of the processes degrading the battery is key to mitigate this effect and advance in the field. We develop a physics-based model to describe degradation during battery cycling under various protocols and storage conditions, with varying check-up (CU) frequencies. The model can disentangle basic degradation mechanisms, such as the growth of the Solid-Electrolyte Interphase (SEI), from silicon mechanisms, such as particle cracking, SEI growth on cracks, and loss of active material (LAM). We investigate the impact of CUs on the observed storage degradation and the reason behind the increased degradation in batteries, including silicon in the anode. Additionally, we relate the observed degradation to operating conditions, enabling future optimization of battery use and design.
summary: This paper presents a physics-based modeling approach to predict the degradation of next-generation lithium-ion batteries using silicon-graphite anodes. By coupling electrochemical and mechanical models, the authors can disentangle how particle cracking and SEI growth contribute to capacity loss. The model successfully captures battery behavior during both active cycling and long-term storage under various temperatures and usage protocols. This work is significant for optimizing the design and usage of high-energy-density batteries.
main_problem: The study aims to understand and model the complex degradation mechanisms, specifically particle cracking and SEI growth, in lithium-ion batteries with silicon-graphite composite anodes during both cyclic and calendar aging.
main_result: The physics-based model accurately predicts capacity loss and silicon health across various temperatures and protocols, identifying that instantaneous SEI growth following crack formation is a dominant degradation driver during check-up cycles.
method: The researchers used a combination of long-term experimental battery testing (up to 96 weeks) and a physics-based Single-Particle-Model with electrolyte effects (SPMe) implemented via the PyBaMM framework.
model_or_system: The system is a lithium-ion battery with a silicon-graphite (Si-Gr) composite anode and an NCA cathode. The model uses coupled partial differential equations to describe SEI growth, mechanical particle cracking, and loss of active material.
key_observables: Capacity loss (CL), State of Health of Silicon (SoH_Si), Loss of Lithium Inventory (LLI), and Loss of Active Material (LAM).
important_parameters: Temperature (20C, 35C, 50C), State of Charge (SoC), Depth of Discharge (DoD), C-rate, and check-up (CU) frequency.
main_assumptions: The model focuses on moderate temperatures (primarily 20C) and assumes thermal dependencies are outside the current scope; it also uses a dimensionality reduction to SPMe for computational efficiency.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of silicon degradation mechanisms; Figure 2 compares simulated vs. measured capacity loss and SoH_Si; Figure 3 breaks down individual degradation contributions; Figures 5-7 show degradation trends across different SoC, DoD, and temperature regimes; Figure 9 shows storage degradation relative to CU frequency.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Si-anode degradation, describes the physics-based model and its implementation, presents validation against cycling and storage experimental data, and concludes with a discussion on model limitations and future directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the degradation mechanisms occurring on silicon: a) continuous SEI growth on the active surface area and tangential stress due to a Li-ion concentration gradient that causes crack opening or growth, b) crack and electrolyte propagation into the particle by battery cycling and consequential silicon island formation, c) instantaneous SEI growth on the surface exposed to electrolyte and LAMSi, d) effective crack description for the model: the crack length lcr and crack width bcr (perpendicular to the illustration) give the area exposed to electrolyte due to cracking, a fraction of the crack-area as additional active area used for Li-ions to intercalate into...
FIG. 2. Simulated versus measured a) CL and b) SoHSi for the cells listed in Tab. I with different temperatures (T = 20°C and T = 35°C) and different cycling protocols (C-rates, mean SoC, DoD).
FIG. 3. a) CL and b) SoHSi of the continuous check-up cycling cell C389. The colored areas in a) describe the different contributions to the simulated CL according to the model.

FIG. 4. a) CL and b) SoHSi of cells cycled in the intermediate SoC region with T = 20°C (C288, C291, C426). The data of the CU cell C389 (black dots) is included to visualize the amount of degradation solely due to the number of performed CUs. Note that these do not refer to the cycled EFCs but correspond to the number of CUs of the cycling cells.

FIG. 5. a) CL and b) SoHSi of cell C250, cycled at elevated SoC. The colored areas in a) describe the different contributions to the simulated CL described by the model. The data of the CU cell C389 (black dots) is included to visualize the amount of degradation solely due to the number of performed CUs. Note that these do not refer to the cycled EFCs but correspond to the number of CUs of the cycling cells.

FIG. 6. a) CL and b) SoHSi of cells cycled in the intermediate SoC region with T = 35°C (C217, C248, C403). The data of the CU cell C389 (black dots) is included to visualize the amount of degradation solely due to the number of performed CUs. Note that these do not refer to the cycled EFCs but correspond to the number of CUs of the cycling cells.

FIG. 7. a) CL and b) SoHSi of cells cycled with high DoD ≥80% (C249, C428, C429). The data of the CU cell C389 (black dots) is included to visualize the amount of degradation solely due to the number of performed CUs. Note that these do not refer to the cycled EFCs but correspond to the number of CUs of the cycling cells.

FIG. 8. a) CL and b) SoHSi of cells with the identical cycling protocol in the intermediate SoC region but varying temper- atures T = 20°C (C426), T = 35°C (C403), and T = 50°C (C406). The data of the CU cell C389 (black dots) is in- cluded to visualize the amount of degradation solely due to the number of performed CUs. Note that these do not refer to the cycled EFCs but correspond to the number of CUs of the cycling cells.

FIG. 9. CL of cells stored at 50% SoC for 96 weeks at T = 20°C with varying CU frequency (every 6/12/96 weeks). The data point for storage with only one CU (blue cross) is used to identify the reference diffusion coefficient of electrons De−,ref. for the continuous SEI growth (see eq. (1)).
FIG. 10. a) CL and b) SoHSi of cells stored for 96 weeks at T = 20°C with different SoCs (10%, 50%, 70%, and 100%) and CUs every 6 weeks.
